text: The video shows a direct hernia on the left side in a seventy-year-old male. The medial umbilical ligament and the hernia sac, approximately three centimeters in size, are visible. The surgeon identifies the triangle of doom and triangle of pain before beginning the dissection.

The video shows a direct hernia on the left side of a seventy-year-old male patient. The medial umbilical ligament is visible, along with a hernia sac approximately three centimeters in size. The surgeon identifies the triangle of doom and triangle of pain before beginning the dissection.
The surgeon begins dissection from the medial umbilical ligament on the left side, using a harmonic device. The iliac vessel is visible, showing pulsation, located just above the arc of the hernia.

The surgeon begins dissection on the left side, starting from the medial umbilical ligament. The iliac vessel is visible, showing pulsation, and the harmonic scalpel is used to dissect above the arc of the hernia.

The surgeon uses cold scissors to gently cut the peritoneum on the left side, separating it from the transversalis fascia. The scissors' jaws break fibrous tissue to create a preperitoneal pocket, staying close to the peritoneum.
The surgeon uses cold scissors to gently cut the peritoneum and separate it from the transversalis fascia on the left side, starting from the medial umbilical ligament.

The surgeon uses scissors to carefully separate the fibrous tissue from the peritoneum, creating a preperitoneal pocket. The dissection continues up to six centimeters lateral to the outer margin of the deep ring towards the ten o'clock position, ensuring the mesh will cover the entire myopectineal orifice.

The surgeon positions the grasper to hold the peritoneum, applying inferior medial traction to allow gas to enter and facilitate dissection of the preperitoneal space. The dissection continues up to six centimeters lateral to the outer margin of the deep ring towards the ten o'clock position, ensuring the mesh will cover the entire myopectineal orifice.
The surgeon uses a grasper to hold the peritoneum, applying inferior medial traction to allow gas to enter and facilitate dissection of the preperitoneal space during the inguinal hernia surgery.
The surgeon uses the back of the scissors as a spatula to separate the peritoneum in an inferior medial direction during the inguinal hernia repair.
The surgeon uses the back of the scissors as a spatula to separate the peritoneum in an inferior medial direction, revealing underlying structures.

The surgeon begins the dissection using a commercially available Covidien placelet for blunt dissection, separating the peritoneum. The dissection focuses on the triangle of pain, starting with lateral dissection followed by medial dissection, aiming to separate the hernia sac. The transversalis fascia is visible, and a pre-peritoneal pocket is created to accommodate a large mesh.

The surgeon begins the dissection using a commercially available placelet from Covidien for blunt dissection to separate the peritoneum. The dissection focuses on the triangle of pain, following the sequence of lateral dissection, then medial dissection, before proceeding to separate the hernia sac.

The surgeon performs lateral dissection of the peritoneum, creating a pre-peritoneal pocket necessary for mesh placement. The transversalis fascia is visible, along with the sigmoid colon and iliac vessel, as the dissection progresses within the triangle of pain.

The surgeon performs a dissection in the triangle of pain, identifying the sigmoid colon and iliac vessel. A lateral dissection of the peritoneum is conducted, carefully keeping the fat away from the peritoneum. The lateral femoral cutaneous nerve (LFC) and genitofemoral nerve (GFN) are visible in the triangle of pain. The dissection is completed without the use of energy devices, using a placelet to separate the peritoneum without bleeding.

The surgeon carefully dissects the peritoneum, keeping the surrounding fat away from the surgical field. The dissection occurs in the triangle of pain, where the lateral femoral cutaneous nerve (LFC) and genitofemoral nerve (GFN) are located. The peritoneum is gradually separated without the use of energy devices, utilizing a placelet for blunt dissection, resulting in no bleeding.

The peritoneum is visibly separating from the preperitoneal fat, completing the lateral dissection within the triangle of pain. The surgeon uses a grasper to stretch the peritoneum inferomedially, preparing to enter the medial dissection phase.
The surgeon uses blunt dissection to separate the peritoneum from the preperitoneal fat, revealing the anatomical structures within the triangle of pain.

The surgeon completes the lateral dissection, preparing to transition to medial dissection. A grasper stretches the peritoneum inferomedially, allowing entry into the medial dissection to expose the Cooper's ligament and the space of Retzius. The transversalis fascia is visible, separating the structures. The Cooper's ligament becomes visible as the surgeon continues to separate the bladder from the retropubic space.

The surgeon exposes the Cooper's ligament and the space of Retzius by separating the transversalis fascia. The Cooper's ligament becomes visible as the dissection progresses medially. The surgeon continues with gentle blunt dissection to separate the bladder from the retropubic space, known as the space of Retzius, while separating the medial peritoneum.
The surgeon performs gentle blunt dissection to separate the medial peritoneum, revealing the opaque transversalis fascia. The peritoneum is pushed down, exposing the space of Retzius.

The surgeon identifies the transversalis fascia, which appears opaque, and observes the peritoneum dented down. The space of Hesselbach is visible, and the dissection proceeds medially away from the midline towards the right side. The Cooper's ligament, also known as the 'lighthouse' due to its pearly white color reflecting light, is clearly visible.

The surgeon dissects medially, moving slightly away from the midline towards the right side, revealing the Cooper's ligament, which is also known as the 'lighthouse' due to its pearly white color reflecting light.

After completing lateral and medial dissection, the surgeon proceeds with sac dissection. The inferior epigastric vessel is visible above, and the peritoneum is held and pulled inferomedially to separate the sac. The transversalis fascia, referred to as the pseudo-sac, is pushed away from the real sac, allowing the vas deferens and spermatic vessels to separate from the peritoneum.

Following the completion of lateral and medial dissection, the surgeon performs sag dissection. The peritoneum is held and pulled inferomedially while the transverse fascia is pushed to facilitate the separation of the vas deferens and spermatic vessels from the peritoneum. The inferior epigastric vessel is visible above, marking the boundary for this dissection.

The surgeon carefully pushes the transversal fascia, also known as the pseudo-sac, away from the real sac, ensuring to stay close to the peritoneum to avoid bleeding. As the peritoneal dissection progresses, the peritoneum visibly sags downward.

The surgeon carefully separates the pseudo-sac from the peritoneum, ensuring to stay close to the peritoneum to avoid bleeding. The peritoneum is dissected, revealing the sagging sac of a direct hernia. The dissection extends towards the triangle of doom, where the peritoneum is separated from the vas deferens and spermatic vessels.

The surgeon inverts the hernia sac, revealing the direct hernia. The sac is clearly visible, and the surgeon extends dissection to the triangle of doom, separating the peritoneum from the vas deferens and spermatic vessels. A preparatory pocket is created with a healthy margin around the defect, using blunt dissection. The inferior epigastric vessel is visible above, and the triangle of doom is below the defect.

The surgeon creates a preparatory pocket with a six-centimeter margin around the hernia defect using blunt dissection. Anatomical landmarks are identified, including the inferior epigastric vessel above and the triangle of doom below. The direct defect is visible, along with the vas deferens and spermatic vessels. The iliac vessel is noted between these structures, and its pulsation is checked to avoid damage during tacker placement.

The surgeon identifies the direct hernia defect, with the vas deferens and spermatic vessels visible. The inferior epigastric vessel is noted, which must be avoided during tacker firing to prevent damage. The iliac vessel is located between these structures, and the dissection is nearly complete. The pocket between the vas deferens and spermatic vessel can be further enlarged, and the pulsation of the iliac vessel is checked.

The surgeon mobilizes the peritoneum, causing the sigmoid colon to drop down over the triangle of doom. The iliac vessel area is visible, and the surgeon performs medial dissection to create a pocket for mesh placement. The vas deferens and spermatic vessels are identified, and the peritoneum is further separated to prevent mesh folding, which could lead to recurrence.

The surgeon mobilizes the peritoneum, causing the sigmoid colon to drop down. The peritoneum is held and further separated over the triangle of doom, revealing the iliac vessel area. The dissection creates a space for mesh placement, with visible anatomical landmarks including the vas deferens and spermatic vessels.

The surgeon further separates the peritoneum to ensure it does not push the mesh upwards. A large pocket is created to prevent folding of the mesh's inferior edge. The sigmoid colon is mobilized downward, and the dissection of the pre-peritoneal space is completed with the removal of the hernia sac.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the pre-peritoneal space and removes the hernia sac. The defect is visible just lateral to the inferior epigastric vessel. A mesh is carefully introduced to cover the entire myopectineal orifice, ensuring it is fully spread without corrugation. The mesh covers the inferior epigastric vessel, vas deferens, spermatic vessel, triangle of doom, triangle of pain, trapezoid of disaster, and extends to the space of radius and Cooper ligament.

The surgeon identifies the hernia defect lateral to the inferior epigastric vessel. A mesh is introduced to cover the entire myopectenial orifice, ensuring it is spread smoothly without corrugation. The mesh covers the inferior epigastric vessel, vas deferens, spermatic vessel, triangle of doom, triangle of pain, trapezoid of disaster, and extends to the space of radius, also covering the Cooper ligament.

The surgeon ensures that the mesh is not folded at any corner and enlarges the space if necessary to cover the entire myopectenial orifice. The Cooper ligament is identified by its hardness, and the first titanium tacker is used to fix the mesh to this ligament for stabilization. Additional tackers are placed over the transversus abdominis muscle and lateral corner, avoiding placement below the inguinal ligament. A tacker is also fixed to the rectus abdominis while taking care to avoid the inferior epigastric vessel.

The surgeon ensures that the mesh is not folded at any corner, enlarging the space if necessary to cover the entire myopectenial orifice. The mesh extends six centimeters around the healthy margin of the defect. The Cooper ligament is identified by its hardness, and the first tacker is fixed here to stabilize the mesh, allowing it to be stretched further.

The surgeon uses a titanium tacker to fix the mesh to the Cooper ligament. The mesh is secured over the transversus abdominis muscle at the lateral corner, above the inguinal ligament. Another tacker is placed to secure the mesh to the rectus abdominis, ensuring the inferior epigastric vessel is not damaged.

The surgeon approximates the lower edge of the peritoneum, lifting it to cover the mesh completely using the double brass technique. The tip of the tacker is felt with the left hand before firing it to secure the peritoneum over the mesh, ensuring no part of the mesh is visible. Suturing may also be performed to enhance coverage, utilizing the peritoneum as an adhesive barrier to prevent adhesion.

The surgeon approximates the lower edge of the peritoneum, lifting it to feel the tip of the tacker before firing it to secure the mesh. The removed peritoneum from the sac provides extra material to cover the mesh effectively, allowing for either the double brass technique or suturing.

The surgeon uses the double brass technique to ensure the lower edge of the peritoneum fully covers the mesh, preventing any part of the mesh from being visible. The peritoneum acts as an adhesive barrier, reducing the risk of adhesions. As the gas is decreased, the peritoneum further reinforces and supports the mesh, ensuring complete coverage.

The surgeon decreases the gas pressure, causing the peritoneum to further expose and reinforce the mesh, ensuring complete coverage of the surgical area. The mesh is now fully covered by the peritoneum, with no visible portions remaining.
The peritoneum fully covers the mesh, ensuring it is not visible, completing the repair of the indirect left-sided inguinal hernia.
